([doing business as](/wiki/Trade_name "Trade name") **Casey's**) is a chain of convenience stores in the [Midwestern](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") and [Southern United States](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"). The company is headquartered in [Ankeny, Iowa](/wiki/Ankeny,_Iowa "Ankeny, Iowa"), a suburb of [Des Moines](/wiki/Des_Moines "Des Moines").[[2]](#cite_note-3) As of October 1, 2023, Casey's had 2,500 stores in 16 states. Following [7-Eleven](/wiki/7-Eleven "7-Eleven")'s purchase of [Speedway](/wiki/Speedway_(store) "Speedway (store)"), Casey's is the third-largest convenience store chain in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") (after 7-Eleven and [Circle K](/wiki/Circle_K "Circle K")/[Couche-Tard](/wiki/Alimentation_Couche-Tard "Alimentation Couche-Tard")) and the largest that is wholly American-owned.[[3]](#cite_note-4) It is one of two Iowa-based [Fortune 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") companies.[[4]](#cite_note-5) Casey's is famous for their [pizza](/wiki/Pizza "Pizza"), including a breakfast pizza and a taco pizza, resulting in Casey's being the fifth largest pizza chain in the U.S.[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7)

**Casey's Retail Company** ([doing business as](/wiki/Trade_name "Trade name") **Casey's**) is a chain of convenience stores in the [Midwestern](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") and [Southern United States](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"). The company is headquartered in [Ankeny, Iowa](/wiki/Ankeny,_Iowa "Ankeny, Iowa"), a suburb of [Des Moines](/wiki/Des_Moines "Des Moines").[[2]](#cite_note-3) As of October 1, 2023, Casey's had 2,500 stores in 16 states. Following [7-Eleven](/wiki/7-Eleven "7-Eleven")'s purchase of [Speedway](/wiki/Speedway_(store) "Speedway (store)"), Casey's is the third-largest convenience store chain in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") (after 7-Eleven and [Circle K](/wiki/Circle_K "Circle K")/[Couche-Tard](/wiki/Alimentation_Couche-Tard "Alimentation Couche-Tard")) and the largest that is wholly American-owned.[[3]](#cite_note-4) It is one of two Iowa-based [Fortune 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") companies.[[4]](#cite_note-5) Casey's is famous for their [pizza](/wiki/Pizza "Pizza"), including a breakfast pizza and a taco pizza, resulting in Casey's being the fifth largest pizza chain in the U.S.[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Casey%27s&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

In 1959, Donald Lamberti leased a [service station](/wiki/Filling_station "Filling station") in [Des Moines, Iowa](/wiki/Des_Moines,_Iowa "Des Moines, Iowa"), from his father. After he had successfully remodeled the station into a convenience store and operated it for nine years, his gasoline supplier and friend, Kurvin C. (K.C.) Fish, suggested that he purchase the Square Deal Oil Company, a service station available for sale in [Boone, Iowa](/wiki/Boone,_Iowa "Boone, Iowa"). Lamberti followed Fish's advice and purchased the station, which he renamed "Casey's" after Fish, then converted it into a convenience store.[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9) The logo on the Boone store was used by the chain until 2020.

The Boone store (located in a town of only 12,500) did well, so Lamberti decided to see if he could duplicate his success. He built another store in [Creston, Iowa](/wiki/Creston,_Iowa "Creston, Iowa") (population 7,000), and that store did well also.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-10) Lamberti became more ambitious and decided to open a store from scratch in the even smaller town of [Waukee, Iowa](/wiki/Waukee,_Iowa "Waukee, Iowa") (population 1,500 at the time). The Waukee store proved to be the most successful of the three, so Lamberti decided to purchase and open more stores, concentrating on towns of less than 5,000 population.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-11)

### 1970s–1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Casey%27s&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1970s–1990s")]

By the late 1970s, the chain had 118 stores and opened its first warehouse.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-11) The original store, operated by Lamberti's parents, entered the Casey's fold in 1977.

In 1982, Casey's opened its first Distribution Center in [Urbandale, Iowa](/wiki/Urbandale,_Iowa "Urbandale, Iowa").[[9]](#cite_note-:0-10)[[10]](#cite_note-:1-11)

Casey's became a publicly traded company in October 1983, with an initial public offering of 700,000 shares. Around this time Casey's began to sell doughnuts and pizza in its stores.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-11) The store also briefly introduced fried chicken before abandoning the endeavor.[[11]](#cite_note-:2-12)

In 1990, Casey's moved to its current facility in Ankeny, Iowa.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-10)

Casey's 1,000th store opened in [Altoona, Iowa](/wiki/Altoona,_Iowa "Altoona, Iowa"), in 1996, the same year Casey's exceeded US$1 billion in annual sales.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-10) In 1998, Lamberti stepped down from his role as CEO and became the company's chairman.[[12]](#cite_note-13) He retired in 2002.[[11]](#cite_note-:2-12)

### 2000s–2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Casey%27s&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2000s–2020s")]

In January 2006, Casey's purchased [Lincoln, Nebraska](/wiki/Lincoln,_Nebraska "Lincoln, Nebraska")-based Gas 'N Shop.[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16) On August 7, 2006, Casey's agreed to purchase the 33-store HandiMart chain, based in [Cedar Rapids, Iowa](/wiki/Cedar_Rapids,_Iowa "Cedar Rapids, Iowa"), in a $63 million deal.[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)

In March 2010, [Alimentation Couche-Tard](/wiki/Alimentation_Couche-Tard "Alimentation Couche-Tard") (operator of several convenience store chains, including [Circle K](/wiki/Circle_K "Circle K")) offered $1.9 billion for control of the company, and later announced a [proxy fight](/wiki/Proxy_fight "Proxy fight") for control. Casey's rejected Couche-Tard's offer and was successful in September 2010 in retaining its board of directors against Couche-Tard's nominated slate.[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20)

While Casey's and Couche-Tard were battling for company control, in September 2010, [7-Eleven](/wiki/7-Eleven "7-Eleven") offered a $2 billion bid for control of the company. Casey's and 7-Eleven engaged in discussions, but in November 2010 elected not to proceed with a merger.[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)

In April 2016, Casey's opened a distribution center in [Terre Haute, Indiana](/wiki/Terre_Haute,_Indiana "Terre Haute, Indiana"), the company's second.[[22]](#cite_note-23) Casey's announced in 2017 that it plans to open about 10 new locations in western Ohio.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

Casey's announced plans in August 2018 to build a store in [Huber Heights, Ohio](/wiki/Huber_Heights,_Ohio "Huber Heights, Ohio").[[24]](#cite_note-25)

In 2018 Casey's announced under increasing pressure from outside investors to adopt a range of new digital initiatives including a new [Fleet Card](/w/index.php?title=Fleet_Card&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fleet Card (page does not exist)"), mobile app, and loyalty program for Casey customers. The CEO announced in late 2018 the initiative would be complete by Quarter 1 of 2020.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

### 2020–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Casey%27s&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2020–present")]

In October 2020, Casey's announced a rebranding, including a new logo and dropping 'General Stores' from its signage (though it remained in the legal corporate name until 2022).[[26]](#cite_note-27)

In November 2020, Casey's announced it was acquiring Omaha-based [Bucky's Convenience Stores](/w/index.php?title=Bucky%27s_Convenience_Stores&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bucky's Convenience Stores (page does not exist)"), a 94 store chain, in a $580 million deal.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

In 2021 Casey's began branding small-footprint stores without kitchens as **goodstop by Casey’s**. This came following a series of acquisitions, and those that were not suitable for a full-service Casey's location were branded as goodstop.[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30)

In April 2021, Casey's opened a third distribution center in Joplin, Missouri.[[30]](#cite_note-31) In May, it closed its acquisition of Buchanan Energy and became the owner of 94 Bucky's Convenience Stores.[[31]](#cite_note-32) As part of the deal, Casey's was ordered to divest six stores to appease regulators.[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Interior_of_a_Casey%27s_Retail_Company_convenience_store_in_Farragut%2C_Tennessee_02.jpg/250px-Interior_of_a_Casey%27s_Retail_Company_convenience_store_in_Farragut%2C_Tennessee_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Interior_of_a_Casey%27s_Retail_Company_convenience_store_in_Farragut,_Tennessee_02.jpg)

Interior of a typical Casey's store

In September 2021, Casey's purchased 38 Pilot Food Mart locations and two truck stop locations from [Pilot Corporation](/wiki/Pilot_Company "Pilot Company"). The locations in [East Tennessee](/wiki/East_Tennessee "East Tennessee"), around the [Knoxville](/wiki/Knoxville,_Tennessee "Knoxville, Tennessee") area, were owned by the Haslam family outright and not part of the company's [national truck stop chain](/wiki/Pilot_Flying_J "Pilot Flying J") jointly owned by Pilot, [Berkshire Hathaway](/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway "Berkshire Hathaway"), and [FJ Management](/wiki/FJ_Management "FJ Management").[[34]](#cite_note-35)

In August 2023, Casey's purchased 63 [Minit Mart](/wiki/Minit_Mart "Minit Mart") and Certified Oil locations in [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky") and [Middle Tennessee](/wiki/Middle_Tennessee "Middle Tennessee"), with plans to convert those stores into Casey's locations; the deal closed in October.[[35]](#cite_note-36)[[36]](#cite_note-37) In November of that year, Casey's expanded into [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") with the purchase of 22 convenience stores mainly in North Texas.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

In July 2024, Casey's purchased Fikes Wholesale, Inc., including its chain of CEFCO convenience stores, in a $1.145B all-cash transaction. The deal, which closed in November 2024, added nearly 200 stores and expanded Casey's further into Texas as well as for the first time into the states of [Alabama](/wiki/Alabama "Alabama"), [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida"), and [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi "Mississippi").[[38]](#cite_note-39)

In September 2024, Casey's purchased 4 stores from Oakcrest Market, expanding its presence in the area of [Fort Smith, Arkansas](/wiki/Fort_Smith,_Arkansas "Fort Smith, Arkansas").[[39]](#cite_note-40)

In October 2024, Casey's purchased the naming rights to the [Wells Fargo Arena](/wiki/Wells_Fargo_Arena_(Des_Moines,_Iowa) "Wells Fargo Arena (Des Moines, Iowa)"), renaming it Casey's Center on July 1, 2025.[[40]](#cite_note-41) The deal cost $18 million and will last for ten years.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

## Operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Casey%27s&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Operations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Brick_Casey%27s_2.png/250px-Brick_Casey%27s_2.png)](/wiki/File:Brick_Casey%27s_2.png)

A Casey's General Store located in the [Amana Colonies](/wiki/Amana_Colonies "Amana Colonies"), Iowa, which complies with 'sparse signage' and building aesthetics ordinances for that area

As of August 2025, Casey's has over 2,600 stores across these states:[[42]](#cite_note-43)

* [Alabama](/wiki/Alabama "Alabama")
* [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas")
* [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida")
* [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois")
